text:
Admission Date:  1997-07-03              Discharge Date:   2004-04-20

Date of Birth:  2013-04-23             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
No Allergies/ADRs on File

Attending:Melissa
Chief Complaint:
STEMI

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Cardiac Catheterization


History of Present Illness:
Mr. Chang is a 52M with PMH of poorly controlled DM2 and
HLD who presents to PCP's office today with intermittent chest
pain and palpitations for past four months. Palpitations are
intermittent but worse when lying flat at night. His usual chest
pain spans across his chest, is sharp, does not radiate and does
not worsen with exertion. He denies nausea or diaphoresis during
these episodes. The patient believes this chest pain has
worsened slightly with the initiation of glipizide at the
beginning of 12. The pain is in the same distribution,
still without radiation, and still not exertional. Patient says
pain does not always come on when he is exercising, and
sometimes occurs when he is sitting in bed. Today, he went to
see PCP regarding this pain, and while rushing to the office
experienced worsened chest pain similar in character to chronic
pain, and felt short of breath. Patient endorses feeling
progressively weaker and more dyspneic over past half a year.
.
At the PCP's office today, he had EKG while he was having active
chest pain with ST elevations in V1-3 per report. He was
immediately sent to the Rush County Memorial Hospital ED.
.
In the ED, initial VS were 98.6 68 121/73 16 97% 2L. Patient was
found to have STEs in V2 only, troponin was negative and CK-MB
was 3. He was given a full-dose aspirin and started on heparin
gtt. After consultation with cardiology attending, patient was
admitted to CCU for close monitoring with plans for cardiac cath
in the morning, or sooner if pt has recurrence of CP or symptoms
overnight.
.
On review of systems, he denies any prior history of stroke,
TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the
time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black
stools or red stools. He denies recent fevers, chills or rigors.
He does note some visual changes which are believed to be
related to his poorly controlled diabetes, progressively
deconditioning/weakness, and claudication (some soreness in his
legs with exertion).
.
Cardiac review of systems is notable for absence of chest pain,
dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea,
ankle edema, palpitations, syncope or presyncope.

Past Medical History:
1. CARDIAC RISK FACTORS: Diabetes, Dyslipidemia
2. CARDIAC HISTORY: none
3. OTHER PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- Positive PPD
- Eczema
- GERD


Social History:
Works as a cab driver. Interested in dietary modification to
improve DM.
- Tobacco history: none
- ETOH: none
- Illicit drugs: none


Family History:
- No family history of early MI, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies,
or sudden cardiac death; otherwise non-contributory.
- Family hx of HTN, DM, and hemorrhoids


Physical Exam:
ADMISSION PHYSICAL EXAM:
VS: T=98.2 BP=127/74 HR=77 RR=11 O2 sat=98(RA)
GENERAL: NAD. Oriented x3. Mood, affect appropriate.
HEENT: NCAT. Sclera anicteric. PERRL, EOMI.
NECK: Supple with JVP of 10 cm.
CARDIAC: RRR, normal S1, S2. No m/r/g. No thrills, lifts. No S3
or S4.
LUNGS: Resp were unlabored, no accessory muscle use. CTAB, no
crackles, wheezes or rhonchi.
ABDOMEN: Soft, NTND. No HSM or tenderness.
EXTREMITIES: No edema, warm.
NEURO: AAOx3, CNII-XII intact, 5/5 strength grossly in upper and
lower extremities, intact sensation to light touch in feet

On discharge exam essentially unchanged.

Pertinent Results:

RDW-13.3 Plt Ct-171

PT-11.1 PTT-35.7 INR(PT)-1.0

AnGap-9
CK(CPK)-361* CK-MB-3 cTropnT-<0.01

Cardiac Catheterization 2004-04-20:
PROCEDURE:
Left heart catheterization was performed via the right radial
artery via
a JACKY catheter through 5 French sheath.
Conscious Sedation:  was provided with appropriate monitoring
performed by
a member of the nursing staff.
HEMODYNAMICS RESULTS	BODY SURFACE AREA:	 m2
 	HEMOGLOBIN:	 gms %
 	REST	 	
**PRESSURES
LEFT VENTRICLE {s/ed}	119/15	 	
AORTA {s/d/m}	119/64	 	
**CARDIAC OUTPUT
HEART RATE {beats/min}	95	 	
RHYTHM	NSR	 	

TECHNICAL FACTORS:
Total time (Lidocaine to test complete) = 18 minutes.
Arterial time = 17 minutes.
Fluoro time = 4.4 minutes.
Effective Equivalent Dose Index = 186 mGy.
Contrast injected:
          Non-ionic low osmolar (isovue, optiray...), vol 86 ml,
Indications - Renal
Premedications:
          Midazolam 1 mg IV
          Fentanyl 25 mcg IV
          ASA 325 mg P.O.
Anesthesia:
          1% Lidocaine subq.
Anticoagulation:
          Heparin 1988 units IV
Other medication:
          Diltiazem bolus 500mcg IA
          Lidocaine 1% 1mL SQ
          Nitroglycerine bolus 100mcg IA
Cardiac Cath Supplies Used:
          - [**Company **], MAGIC TORQUE 180CM
          - ALLEGIANCE, CUSTOM STERILE PACK
          - MERIT, LEFT HEART KIT
          - TERUMO, JACKY RADIAL CATHETER
          5FR TERUMO, GLIDESHEATH
          - TERUMO, TR BAND LARGE


COMMENTS:
1.  Selective coronary angiography of this right-dominant system
demonstrated no angiographically apparent flow-limiting coronary
artery
disease.  The LMCA, LAD, LCx, and RCA had no signficiant
stenoses.
2.  Left ventriculography revealed normal LV systolic function
(EF >
55%) with no mitral regurgitation.
3.  Limited resting hemodynamics revealed normal systemic
arterial
pressures with a central aortic SBP of 119mmHg.  Left
ventricular
filling pressure was mildly elevated with an LVEDP of 15mmHg.
There was
no gradient across the aortic valve on pullback from the left
ventricle
to the ascending aorta.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
1. No angiographically apparent flow-limiting coronary artery
diseae.
2. Normal ventricular function.
3. Mildly elevated left-sided filling pressure.
4. Normal systemic arterial pressures.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. Chang is a 52M with PMH of poorly controlled DM2 and
HLD who presented to his PCP's office with intermittent chest
pain and palpitations for past four months, found to have STE in
V1-3 but negative cardiac enzymes.
.
# ATYPICAL CHEST PAIN: Patient has had chronic chest pain for
past four months, worse today on exertion while going to PCP's
office and associated with dyspnea.  Patient was evaluated in ED
and found to have negative troponin x 2, but due to story
consistent with unstable angina, severity of EKG changes and
patient's poorly controlled diabetes leading to possible
atypical presentation, patient was admitted to the CCU. He was
started on heparin gtt with goal PTT 60-100 sec, Aspirin 325mg
daily, and cardiac catheterization was undertaken. On cath,
there was no evidence of significant coronary artery disease. As
Mr. Chang Conner relief of his chest pain with maalox,
and the pain is worse with recumbancy, GERD was seen as the most
likely etiology. He was discharged with ranitidine.
.
# DM: Patient takes glipizide and pioglitazone at home. Oral
hypoglycemics were held and insulin sliding used during
hospitalization.
.
# HLD: Continued home simvastatin 40
.
# GERD: patient has chronic reflux symptoms, does not take any
medications at home. On admission, he endorses severe gas pains
in abdomen radiating from epigastric area down to LLQ. Maalox
was given for reflux symptoms and gas relief. He was discharged
with ranitidine.
.
# Metabolic Alkalosis: Patient had bicarb 33 on admission.
Differential included emesis, diuretic use, or OSA leading to
respiratory acidosis and chronic metabolic compensation.
Alkalosis resolved the following morning.


Medications on Admission:
- Pioglitazone (ACTOS) 30mg PO daily -- Patient states he does
not take.
- Glipizide 10mg PO DAILY
- Simvastatin 40 mg Oral Tablet

Discharge Medications:
1. aspirin 81 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig: One (1)
Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO once a day.
Disp:*30 Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.)(s)* Refills:*0*
2. ranitidine HCl 150 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO twice a
day.
Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
3. simvastatin 40 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
4. glipizide 10 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day.
5. Maalox Maximum Strength 400-400-40 mg/5 mL Suspension Sig:
07-19 mL PO four times a day as needed for heartburn.
Disp:*200 mL* Refills:*0*


Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
Type II Diabetes
Hyperlipidemia


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.


Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. Chang,
    You were admitted with chest pain which we were initially
concerned may have been related to your heart. We performed a
cardiac catheterization which did not demonstrate any lesion
which would have caused a heart attack or angina. It is likely
that the symptoms you had are related to your reflux (heartburn)
disease. It is important to avoid heavy lifting of your right
arm for 4 days.

The following medication changes have been made:
START Maalox as needed for reflux symptoms (upper abdominal
pain)
START ranitidine daily (take whether you have reflux symptoms or
not, this drug will prevent reflux)
START Aspirin 81mg daily to prevent heart attacks (your diabetes
puts you at increased risk)

Otherwise continue the medications which were prescribed to you
before you came to the hospital. It is critical to your health
to take all your medications as prescribed.

Followup Instructions:
